speaker: interviewer
text: An interview with Eva Weiss, November 16, 1995. Tape number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Weiss, you've taken us now to 1944. What happened in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, the only thing I remember immediate, our own tragedy, was when my mother was very sick, and she was operated on-- a hysterectomy, which was I think in February of '44. And by that time, of course, my sister is home and I'm home. And my mother decided and my father that if I can't go to a higher school, maybe try the following year and there maybe, you know, things would change. They would accept us. Or go to a convent, a Catholic school. And they would take a few more Jews than the 3%, with high money, you know, Jewish money. You had to pay yourself. And at that point, I told my father, no, I'm not going to be with the nuns in their school. That doesn't appeal to me. So they decided-- we had a cousin in Satoraljaujhely, which is about 25 to 30 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that, please? As close as you can.

speaker: interviewee
text: Satoraljaujhely. That was a main point of our life, because of the closest largest city. S-A-T-O-- all right, you know what? I can't even spell it. It's just if I write it. Satoraljaujhely. Let me write it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Here.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I write, then I can spell it, because it's such a long name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you pronounce it again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shator-oya-OO-ee-hay.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it in relation to like Budapest or another large city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was completely other end.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely. Budapest was-- we were like, what-- southwest, and it was completely-- and it was a border of Czechoslovakia. S-A-T-O-R-A-L-J and then capital U-J-H-E-L-Y. And I would go learn cosmetic and beauty. Be a beautician or something. So I did this like about only two months, and I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tickets, like, you know, season ticket which you buy for a whole month, because it was cheaper. And you went early in the morning. And I would walk to the station, which was at least two, three kilometers, a long walk. So we walked, and take the train and be there like-- took us an hour by train. And this particular-- and I did this January, February-- and this particular day, which is in March--

speaker: interviewee
text: --1944, Where my mother is home, and we are thankful, and, you know, she's out of the hospital. And we all just were busy taking care of her. Their 25th anniversary was end of March. And my sister and I decided, and my sister already looked up to me that I'm maybe a little bit grown up. And she's coming in 12 o'clock train, and we're going to have to look for something special to buy, probably silver or something. And that particular day, I think it was on a Monday-- yes, it was a Monday-- I do my routine to Satoraljaujhely. We get off the train, and we can't go anywhere. The riders, they push all the people who get off in the station into the station, inside the room, and they stop it. And all of a sudden soldiers, Germans, all over. And, you know, as a child we were not scared. I didn't know what the heck was going on. We were looking at them. They were good-looking boys and young faces and all. And it's in chaos and turmoil. And all the trains came from this direction, from the east. The other train would come from the west direction. All the small towns would go into this school from different villages, towns, because that was already a large community and a large Jewish community also. And people were not like in a village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I couldn't get further and, of course, I didn't know if my cousin knew what happened to me. Did I come in or not? And there we are with all the kids, with the Gentile kids and all who go to school to that point. We're just talking and gathering together and watching all the chaos. And this was like, you know, you arrive every morning there by nine, and we are there 12 o'clock. The train comes in, and which we called express train, and my sister gets off. And I couldn't believe my eyes. In Olaszliszka, which was not even two kilometers, did they not know that Hitler was already in [INAUDIBLE]? So my sister gets off, and she was-- I have to tell you that she was very, well, loved and catered and watched over, because she was a beautiful girl. And my father always just watched that anything-- no harm should come to her. And when she gets off the train, and she sees me, and I holler, Esther, what are you doing here? You know, and she says Eva, why are you here? Why are you not, you know, where you're supposed to be at our cousin's? So then she looks around, sees it. And she had sense enough that they finally let another train from the opposite direction coming back to our town Olaszliszka, and she had the express sticker and a round trip, so she could just jump on it and go home. Well, that's what she did. She wants to take me with her. I said no, I'm not paying extra. I'm not going to. I have my ticket, you know, season ticket.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get home that day until 12 o'clock. And by that time, of course, my sister gets home all right, and they are not in Liszka yet. Hitler is not in Liszka. Nobody knows. She goes home and tells them what's going on, and my father finds out that she left me at the station. Well, I am sure she got hell, but I did, I came home with all the kids who are commuting back to Liszka-- 12 o'clock-- and I was the only Jewish kid. Nothing happened. You know, they didn't say nothing. They didn't harm me. And I was, of course, by that time glad to be home. My father was at the gate waiting for me, because they knew the last train was coming out of [INAUDIBLE] at 11:30 or so. So we were, you know, like 12 o'clock getting home and walk home. I think he was ready to kill me. He was. He hugged me, then slapped me. I remember getting two slaps on my face and my mother crying and hugging me that I finally safe. And that's when I know my whole life was turned upside down. Then I knew. And then, of course, about few days later, the Germans arrive in Hungary. And the first thing, you know, they go at the-- what do we call it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, come on. I can't even think of it. They go with a loud speaker, the gendarme, which is a [INAUDIBLE]. That was the Hungarian police. They come out to the center of the town with their loud speaker. No Jews allowed to step out. And Jew's curfew not to walk out of the house, and if they do, they are allowed out from 10 to five. And they must wear a yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what town was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our town. Hitler is all over it, OK, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a question about the train station. What was causing the chaos?

speaker: interviewee
text: The chaos of the soldiers marching in. See, the Gentiles didn't know what was going on either. The same way we know that soldiers will come to us. The Jews knew that, OK? They finally believed that this is what we heard, this is all the horror stories, and here it is in our town. But there was the chaos when you're at a train station, and every 10 minutes the train comes from this direction, that direction, because that was the center of the connection. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If we were going anywhere, to Budapest, you would transfer in that town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the trains were coming in and the soldiers start to, you know, stop the people. You can't go further. You can't get out to the town. You just get out of the train or go back further if you're on it. And just keep like a student-- hundreds of students-- keeping in a station all day long. Not a one, even a Gentile, not a one could leave further.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a chaos. It wasn't organized. Nothing was organized. But the whole shocking truth was-- the shocking thing to realize that they cut the wires. They cut the telephone wires. They cut radio station. And one time did not know that they were already in other town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no communication. That's why it was the chaos. And if somebody said something, the next guy didn't want to believe it, because they didn't confirm it by radio or by telephone. The Germans cut the wires. There was no communication. So now, what month is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in end of March.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, 1944. Towards the end of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so now you had to wear the yellow arm bands.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then we have to wear the yellow arm bands. So this was coming in like during the week. And every night another horror story would come on the loud speaker for Jews to do this and Jews to do that. And we knew that they were after the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what? What kinds of things were they asking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not to go out. We're not allowed to go to the open market. Jews were not allowed to go to open market. Jews were not allowed to go to stores. OK? And, like, you were confined like you were a prisoner in your own home. Because you couldn't go to the store, even if you had the money to buy your ration, because by that time sugar, flour, meat, everything was rationed, because the war was going on. So if you had something in the home, you ate. And if not, you were out of luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this went on a few days and, of course, the butcher shop did not open. My father didn't go in. Four or five days later-- I think it was four days later-- they appear in our house. The gendarme with the German solider and tells my father that you get yourself to the slaughter house and make sure that the town people and the German soldiers will have meat. And if you have the cattle on hand, which you're going to kill, you do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, see, my father that week, that last three days, he didn't even think of it. He didn't care. He didn't even want to go to the slaughter house. But then they came. And my mother was convalescing, you know, and she was still in bed. And I remember the gendarme comes in, and my mother is laying down. And my father must have been outside, at the butcher shop or someplace or in the barn. He's not inside the house. I'm only there and my little brother. And they go from room to room, and they start looking around-- money or jewelry or whatever. And they open up every drawer and my poor mother just in bed. Then in the end-- and I'm going into the bedroom where she was laying-- in the end, the soldier tells, I think, the Hungarian police, get her out of bed. And he says at first, roughly, he says, get out of bed. And my mother says, I can't. I am very sick. I just had an operation.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the German will not accept this. And he takes the gun, the shotgun, puts it on the bed, lifts up the mattress like-- and literally lifts my mother in the air. Just she-- she fell off the bed, and she faints.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, my father comes in. And I thought my father was going to kill both of them right there. He had a Hungarian temper, and by that time he was very strained, and he could hardly hold his temper in all this. He grew up that he was born and his grandfather and everything is going chaos. And this was the first bad experience that I noticed at the time. And, of course, this went on like two weeks, or not even two weeks. All I know is there was-- a day after Pesach, OK, which was in April, beginning of April. And then about four days-- all right, so anyway, and then I have to go to trace back this, because I want you to understand how naive we were. As the German comes in, and my father sees what's going on, he picks up my sister. He picks my sister up and pays off our-- because we had Gentiles all around us. It wasn't just Jews, Jews. Gentiles were very close neighbors and pays him. And with the wagon they put my sister in the wagon, a bunch of hay on top of her and took them to another little town to a very good friend of ours who is maybe 10 kilometers, not even that much, thinking that they're not going to be that town, and my sister is going to be safe. That's how naive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the law came, then you had to wear the yellow stars, and everything. I went out the first day, all the way down to my girlfriend's house-- the Gentiles-- and back and no star. I said, I'm not going to put it on. I was a little chutzpah, and I was like my father's daughter. I did not want to obey right away what they wanted me to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walked down all the way, which she lived-- see, this was just one main street, the whole Liszko, where mostly the Jews lived and merchants and some Gentiles. They were already well-to-do Gentiles, not the real peasant. And I went all the way on that main street and back. And it was about five o'clock. I knew we had to be home five, so I'm rushing back by five. And I don't know what made me do it or why even. Don't ask me, because I knew I wasn't probably welcome in there, but just chutzpah, I wanted to walk down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father sees me again. He says, Eva [INAUDIBLE]. You know, that's how they call me, Eva [INAUDIBLE] when it's like a nickname as a child. Eva [INAUDIBLE]. Why are you doing this thing? Why did you go out? Where is you yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, apu-- this is short for Daddy-- apu, [HUNGARIAN]. I'm not going to put it on. And he looked at me and says, you put it on right now. You are Jewish girl, and you cannot walk out. Don't start any problem for us. So, OK, I obeyed of course, and then I had to put on the stars.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when my sister the following day gets shipped out to this street, the news comes, the loudspeaker again goes on, you know, at the center of the town just like a court-- the city hall-- front of the city hall it was always. And, of course, every Jew, they don't want to listen. They're inside their home actually to listen, because they wouldn't even go out to the square. We're going to be gathered together, and it's a Sunday morning. And we're going into our synagogue. Olaszliszka had two synagogues. One was about four houses away from us, or three houses, and one was where the rabbinical family belonged. You know, the real orthodox, like a [INAUDIBLE], OK? So, in a large synagogue all the Jews with suitcases, what they can carry, has to gather to be there, OK? You can allow to take-- I think we took like blankets. I don't even know-- pillow or blanket. I don't know. And one suitcase.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now the problem is get my sister back, as she is in a little town. So he had to make arrangement and hide and get her back in the wagon somehow. I don't even know how they got her back. And she's back in, you know, our town.

speaker: interviewee
text: But even I think back to those little incidents, those scary things. And she says goodbye to my mother and all, thinking that-- I don't know what we were thinking. I mean, it was so naive and so stupid to realize that if they're in here, why wouldn't they be in the next town? Well, what happened when you were all gathered when your sister came back, and you were all gathered in the synagogue, where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when we were gathered, all the Jews gathered with the family, children, mother, father-- everybody-- sick ones, old ones, all of them in a synagogue. And we stayed there all night, and then in a synagogue they would divide like, I don't know, maybe with a curtain or something. And even when you're dressed-- so when we're going and we know that we have to be there, this was I think on a Saturday-- on a Saturday, and we stayed all day Saturday in the synagogue and slept on a floor. Well, we'd get dressed, as many clothes as we can, so I put on all my underwear, layers of slips, you know. I think two, three skirts-- anything, but I thought it was good-- and my winter coat. And it's already almost spring. And the same with my sister and my mother, and we packed. And nobody had that many suitcases. So they made bundles. This is my clothing, and that was my sister, my brother. And all the Jews are in a synagogue. In a synagogue, they take the men, strip them to naked. That's already with the Hungarian police and a German solider directing and being there. And at one point, I think my sister was a little bit further away from me, and they get to me. My mother is stripped and standing there so weak and so sickly. And I'm a young girl, and I'm sick. And I don't want to take my panties off. I do not want to take it off. And I had my, you know, thing on-- what they call it-- the sanitary belt. And he-- the gendarme, the Hungarian gendarme-- goes, take-- in Hungarian tells me roughly, take your pants off. And I says, I can't. He says, why. And I tell him, I'm sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother hears my voice, and I'm already crying and I'm blushing, and I used to be very, very shy and easily blushed. She somehow hears my voice, turns around as naked as she's standing there, and grabs me and hollers. Can't you see she's an innocent child? She's embarrassed to take it off.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he puts his hand right between my leg, right between my leg to see if I hide jewelry or anything there. I thought that minute I wanted the floor to open up, and I think I'll just disappear. As a 15-year-old girl, naked, seeing my mother naked. And they just-- you know, I don't know if they ever found on anybody anything, but this is what they did. They were very rough. They were as rough as Germans, and that's why I hate them, actually hate them sometimes more than I hate the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a very, very bad experience. And from then on, my mother just clinging to me, holding my hand, because I was a child and felt I can't even take care of myself. And my sister-- I think at that point, my mother didn't notice, but she's halfway dressing up, and they let you put-- after a while, they checked you, stripped you, touched you, then they let you put back the clothing. So we put back the clothing, and that's how we go.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day is Sunday, the church bell going. And the people are going to church. And, of course, the Jews are in their synagogue, already gathered. And thinking back now, I started to talk to my brother-- my cousin about it-- that recollect my thought that they didn't do it while church was going on, that the Gentiles cannot see us, but they did like two o'clock afternoon. The wagons would show up with the gendarme on it and gather everybody. You were allowed to stay together as families. So as many of us can-- we were six of us-- so we took care of one wagon. Oh, I have to track back. In the meantime this is going on, Saturday afternoon, my brother from Budapest, the oldest one, Laszlo, shows up. Well, we thought-- mother was thrilled to see here son-- but we thought this was the end of the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not know when they gave him a furlough, because he was legally drafted with the Hungarian-- it was an army already-- that was just labor camp, but drafting, OK? Had to appear at that so-and-so station, and they gave him three days furlough to go home to say goodbye to the parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, we gathered already to go to the ghetto. He does not know that. This is no communication, nothing. We didn't even know what happened to them in our whole two weeks or so. And no mail came, nothing. So when my brother shows up, and he sees what's going on, he does not want to go back. My father, I think that was the biggest argument in my life that I witnessed between my father, my brother, and my mother crying, that she says you go, you go back to Budapest. You go on your station, because-- he shook him, and I think even wanted to slap him, but he was six feet tall already. And he says you go, because you are the one who's going make it. You are the one who's going to stay alive. If you don't go, I go right now to the station house and report you. And my oldest brother was very devoted to my mother. I knew this. He just did not want to go. He wanted to go with us to the ghetto. We didn't know it's called the ghetto, but this is where we are going, and we were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is Sunday afternoon. We are going with the wagon, and my brother is still home. He stays in the house, and they let you take-- they told you-- my mother even cleaned the kitchen, I remember, before we went into the synagogue. And my brother stays in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: They tell you not to lock the doors, but you must take care of your pets. The dogs, cats-- any Jewish dogs, cats from families have to be killed. Well, we had a dog, and he was so marvelous and my father's right hand, going with him, following the wagon every time to the slaughterhouse, where my father would put-- go into the summer kitchen we had, where all his boots were and pants, you know, the certain clothes he wore there-- and bloody. When that dog would smell it and know it that my father is changing, he would just run around. And if he came home 12 o'clock before he opened the gate, because we had like a gate and a long yard, but the homes were close to each other, so it wasn't really a light where flowers could grow. Further down, that's where they had flowers growing. And that dog would be just outside, and going into the kitchen, to the house, we had five steps. He would never cross the top steps. He would just lay on the top step but never get into the house. So he was an outside dog, and he always slept in the barn-- winter, summer, that was his bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father wouldn't kill him, no way he'll kill. His name was Peszi. He was a beautiful white dog with the brown spots. And that day Peszi stays, and my brother stays at the gate and watching this procedure, when going up they started at one end of the town where the family of the rabbis lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put him on a wagon, and we're already just approaching our wagon, and we see the wagon going by with all the rabbi's family. Their name was [INAUDIBLE]. And the rabbi was an older rabbi but distinguished with the little-- not a long beard, just up to here-- and with a kittel, you know, the white kittel on, and a white yarmulke on. At that point, my brother saw this, he run to that, out of the thing. His door was-- I mean, the gate was open, and he's standing there and watching all this and stomaching it. And he run-- not our wagon, because we were not loaded yet, but the rabbi's wagon. And just ran, jumped up the wagon and grabbed his hand and kissed them. And the gendarme noticed this, because one was always walking behind a wagon and one was sitting who was handling the horses, you know, and noticed that. And he gave him with his pistol, with his shotgun, a clop on his head that I thought he was going to die right there and faint. And he just fell down, and then he is on the ground. And when my father saw this, I don't think-- I don't even know what was the reaction. I think we all just screamed or cried. And then we were loading up, but they started at one end of the town, and then that's how they went. And we were loading up, and my brother stayed, of course, behind and that was the last thing we knew of him. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to Satoraljaujhely, which was a big Jewish community. And that's where they made the ghetto. What happened there, that all the Jews who used to live-- wasn't a ghetto, but let's say confined more to 10 blocks of Jewish people, they emptied it. They emptied their furniture. They confiscated everything, and they took that 10 blocks or so and more like-- it wasn't a desirable area, more towards the gypsies where they would be end of the town, outskirts of the town, and emptied that, and they put like four or five families to one house. That's how a ghetto was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't barracks or anything. It was a regular building, regular thing. And we were somehow lucky that we actually wind up in the same building where our cousin lived in Satoraljaujhely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we went there-- they were younger than my parents-- they had beautiful furniture. The furniture was out of the room, and they shoved us two families-- two or three of us-- Weiss family, which had seven of us. We are six, two sisters, two young brothers, and my father-- about 14, 15 people to one room. And whatever we had with us, we just laid it on the floor, and that's where we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the ghetto was also terrible, crazy chaos, because they didn't know what the heck they're going to do with us or what's going to happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happen is, you were allowed to take as much as you can carry food-- that I remember. So my mother would make in a jar-- that I remember distinctly-- a base, a soup base, just like we buy it in cubes now, they made at home with the schmaltz and flour and seasoning. And they put it in a big jar, because in Europe in a small town you stored everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was still kind of spring and winter, so you had plenty of fat and plenty of flour, because we never went hungry, no matter how the [INAUDIBLE] was, my father always, always managed to put food on a table. And we had the bread and the challah and all this. But there, when they knew that we're going, I remember my mother and my sister, she was so weak. And then she organized to make a lot of this paste, put it in a big jar, and we carried that with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get into the ghetto. There's no ration of food, nothing, just what you were carrying. And all of a sudden, two, three days later, they notice there is no food for all these people. But this just wasn't Olaszliszka. This was the state, like Michigan state, OK, like we are in Detroit, Michigan. There was [INAUDIBLE], so all the whole [INAUDIBLE]-- villages, smaller town, all the surrounding towns, arrived to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they had to take I think about four or five more streets. And then, of course, comes the law that you're not allowed to go from this surrounding. Your area was like 10, 15-- I think it was about 15 blocks. But we were very cramped. Hundreds and hundreds of Jews. I mean, you didn't know each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
